retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Low vision, visual impairment, and blindness are broad terms encompassing a range of conditions affecting an individual's ability to see and function in daily life. Low vision describes those whose vision cannot be fully corrected by glasses, contact lenses, refractive surgery, or other surgery. Visual impairment describes those whose decreased visual function interferes with the ability of one to perform their activities of daily living. However, the majority of causes of vision impairment can be prevented or rectified. As aging occurs, more and more blindness cases are added to the global burden, leading to an increased demand for eye healthcare services. The leading causes of blindness globally are cataracts, glaucoma, uncorrected refractive errors, age-related macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy. This activity provides an in-depth exploration of the various facets of blindness, low vision, and visual impairment, with a special emphasis on causes, legal interpretations, and the role of the interprofessional healthcare team in managing these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Presbyopia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Presbyopia is a prevalent visual impairment affecting around 1.8 billion individuals worldwide. [19] [23] By 2030, this number is expected to increase to more than 2.1 billion. [24] This condition is typical and physiological in individuals older than 50, although the precise age of start and severity differ among populations. Research indicates that presbyopia generally manifests symptomatically between the ages of 40 and 45, with nearly universal prevalence by age 60. [25] Studies have shown that more than 80% of individuals aged 40 develop presbyopia. [24] The worldwide prevalence of presbyopia is escalating as a result of an aging population and growing life expectancy. By 2050, more than 20% of the world's population is expected to be older than 60, which will have a significant impact on this condition in the near future. [26] In numerous high-income nations, the percentage of adults older than 65 is increasing swiftly, resulting in a concomitant rise in the incidence of presbyopia. The ongoing increase in smartphone and digital screen usage highlights the need to manage presbyopia, given the heightened pressure on close vision tasks in both professional and recreational contexts. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Low Vision Aids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: According to the World Health Organization, there are at least 2.2 billion people globally with visual impairment. [3] Out of these, at least 1 billion could have been prevented from vision impairment with timely intervention. Among these 1 billion, uncorrected refractive errors are responsible for most cases, accounting for 88.4 million. [4] Cataracts that can be reversed with a minor surgery comprised another 94 million, glaucoma 7.7 million, corneal opacity 4.2 million, diabetic retinopathy 3.9 million, trachoma 2 million, and uncorrected presbyopia 826 million. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to WHO, approximately 285 million people have a visual impairment, including 39 million who are blind and 246 million who have low vision. Of those who are blind, 90% live in developing countries. For each blind person worldwide, an average of 3.4 people have low vision, with the country and regional variation ranging from 2.4 to 5.5 people. [10] [13] According to another report, 180 million people worldwide are visually disabled, including 45 million who are blind (80% of this blindness is preventable). [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: More than 82% of all people who are blind are 50 years of age and older. Due to the expected number of years lived in blindness, childhood blindness is a significant problem, with an estimated 1.4 million children under age 15 who are blind. Females have a significantly higher risk of being visually impaired than males. [15] Approximate prevalence of blindness due to various causes: Cataracts: 19 million Glaucoma: 6.4 million Trachoma: 5.6 million Childhood blindness: >1.5 million Other causes: 10 million [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Low Vision Aids -- Issues of Concern -- Non-availability of Low Vision Aids Services
retrieved_docs.content: The countries or areas with >10% coverage had a higher proportion of older people and a more urbanized population. The survey also revealed that LVA services were primarily monodisciplinary and were available at secondary and tertiary eye care levels. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A sizeable portion of the blind population (32%) is between 40 and 70 years old, and 60% is over 60 years old. The yearly incidence of blindness due to cataracts is 2 million cases. According to the national survey analysis on blindness 2001-2002, the estimated prevalence of blindness is 8.5% in the over-50 age group and 1.1% in the general population. Approximately 7% of children are suffering from ocular conditions leading to low vision and problems with eyesight. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Many international, national, and local organizations are working to bring more awareness about vision and eye health, provide access to healthcare, and fight blindness. Research is being done worldwide to study the etiologies of many eye conditions causing permanent vision loss and to develop a proper treatment to control or cure these conditions. WHO is the leader in monitoring trends, raising awareness, and coordinating efforts to fight blindness. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Low Vision Aids -- Issues of Concern -- Non-availability of Low Vision Aids Services
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of LVA services in a region does not necessarily mean good coverage of services. A survey in 195 countries revealed that only 115 had coverage of LVA services; among 115, 39% had <10% coverage, 22 had coverage between 11 to 50%, and 8 had coverage of more than 50%. Only 23 countries had coverage in the Asia Pacific region, and five countries had >10% coverage.

retrieved_docs.title: Low Vision Aids -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the functions of low vision aids. Summarize the clinical significance of low vision aids. Identify the concerns of low vision aids. Explain the classification of low vision aids. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Visual Acuity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are many reasons that an individual could have an altered visual acuity. One of the most common causes of altered vision is a refractive error, such as myopia (nearsightedness) and hyperopia (farsightedness). Additional causes of visual impairment include astigmatism, amblyopia, retinal detachment, macular degeneration, ischemia, cataracts, glaucoma, corneal abrasion, or other traumatic injuries. Visual acuity testing is essential as many of these factors can be benefited from early intervention. It is crucial to determine an individual's best-corrected visual acuity. The WHO describes individuals with low vision as having a best-corrected vision of 20/60 or worse, and blind as best corrected vision worse than 20/400, whereas legal blindness is identified as 20/200 in the United States. These formal definitions can have ramifications when it comes to accommodations and abilities to operate a motor vehicle. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Presbyopia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Presbyopia is a common, age-related condition that progressively reduces the eye's ability to focus on close objects, affecting nearly all adults older than 40. As global demographics evolve towards an aging population, the prevalence of presbyopia and its effects on the quality of life and visual function are becoming progressively substantial. This activity provides a comprehensive review of the etiology, clinical manifestations, and advancing therapeutic approaches for presbyopia. In addition, it offers evidence-based insights into the anatomical and physiological changes of the aging eye, accompanied by a summary of diagnostic methods and a comprehensive range of both surgical and nonsurgical treatment options, including corrective lenses, pharmacological agents, and lens-based interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Blindness -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: WHO also defines blindness as visual acuity of 3/60 (Snellen’s chart) or less or its equivalent (see Table. WHO and the National Programme on Control of Blindness [NPCB] classification of blindness). In the absence of visual acuity charts, when paramedical staff takes the visual acuity, it is defined as the inability to count fingers at a distance of 3 meters in daylight to indicate 3/60 or its equivalent. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Low Vision Aids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: World health organization (WHO)  international classification of diseases (ICD-10) defines a person with low vision as one with visual acuity between 6/18-3/60 in the good eye and field of vision between 20 and 30 degrees. [6] The WHO working definition of LVA (Bangkok, 1992) defines a person with low vision as one who has a visual impairment or visual functional impairment even after treatment of the ocular pathology and or refractive error correction with a vision of less than 20/60 to a perception of light or a visual field of fewer than 10 degrees from the point of fixation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Prescribing Glasses for Presbyopia -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: The national services provided can be greatly influenced by economic status. [29] The improved standard of health care delivery and the rapid technological advancement in the 21st century has heralded longer life expectancy worldwide. Consequently, chronic diseases like glaucoma and age-related macular degeneration are on the rise. Diabetic and hypertensive retinopathy, as well as presbyopia, is expected to increase. [30] Therefore, near visual impairment resulting from uncorrected presbyopia may affect daily activities and quality of life, which may lead to decreased productivity among individuals. [31]

Additional specific sub‑conditions listed under the same category are انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي, تنكس حصوي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. Together, these entities illustrate the extensive and heterogeneous nature of choroidal and retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of precise classification for effective patient care, targeted research, and accurate medical coding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the retina, reinforcing that retinal involvement—whether from systemic metabolic disorders, primary retinal diseases, or targeted retinal lesion destruction—requires precise identification of the retinal site for accurate diagnosis and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253

Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2371
retrieved_docs.content: Intracapsular extraction of lens is a specific type of lens and cataract procedure, representing one of the surgical techniques used to remove the crystalline lens in cataract surgery. This procedure falls under the broader category of lens and cataract procedures, which encompass various methods for addressing lens opacities and related ocular conditions. Understanding that intracapsular extraction of lens is a subset of lens and cataract procedures helps clinicians select appropriate surgical approaches and informs patient counseling about the range of available treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634

Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation.
